questions.question: Briefly explain the purpose of the Program Counter (PC) register in instruction sequencing.
keyPoints: Holds the memory address of the next instruction to be fetched and executed.
Automatically increments to point to the next sequential instruction after each fetch.
Can be updated to a non-sequential address during jump or branch instructions to alter the flow of execution.

questions.question: Describe the basic steps of the fetch-decode-execute cycle and explain the role of the Instruction Register (IR) in this process.
keyPoints: Fetch: The instruction is retrieved from memory at the address in the Program Counter (PC).
Decode: The control unit interprets the instruction opcode to determine the operation to perform.
Execute: The control unit generates the necessary signals to perform the operation (e.g., ALU, memory access).
The Instruction Register (IR) holds the current instruction being decoded and executed after it is fetched from memory.

questions.question: Compare and contrast the characteristics of a Reduced Instruction Set Computer (RISC) and a Complex Instruction Set Computer (CISC) architecture. Discuss how these differences impact instruction format, the instruction set, and the complexity of the control unit.
keyPoints: RISC uses a small, highly optimized set of simple instructions, while CISC uses a larger set of more complex, multi-cycle instructions.
RISC typically employs a fixed-length instruction format for simpler decoding, whereas CISC uses variable-length instructions to encode complex operations.

RISC requires more instructions (and a compiler) to perform a complex task, but executes them very quickly (often one per clock cycle). CISC aims to complete a task in fewer instructions, but executes them more slowly.
